questions.text: Why do we need elections?
options: To choose who will make laws
To choose who will form the government
To choose which party's policies will guide the government
questions.explanation: Elections are a democratic way for people to choose their representatives and the path of the country.

questions.text: What is the minimum age to vote in India?
questions.explanation: According to the principle of Universal Adult Franchise, every citizen aged 18 and above can vote.

questions.text: The country is divided into different areas for purposes of elections. These are called:
questions.explanation: A constituency is a specific area where voters elect one representative.

questions.text: For Lok Sabha elections, India is divided into how many constituencies?
questions.explanation: There are 543 elected seats in the Lok Sabha.

questions.text: The representative elected from each Lok Sabha constituency is called:
questions.explanation: MP stands for Member of Parliament.

questions.text: For State Assembly elections, the elected representative is called:
questions.explanation: MLA stands for Member of Legislative Assembly.

questions.text: What is a 'Reserved Constituency'?
options: A constituency where only someone belonging to SC or ST can contest
A constituency for the ruling party
questions.explanation: The Constitution provides for reservations to ensure weaker sections have a fair share of representation.

questions.text: The list of those who are eligible to vote is officially called the:
questions.explanation: The Electoral Roll is updated regularly to include new voters and remove those who have passed away.

questions.text: What is the minimum age to be a candidate for elections in India?
questions.explanation: While you can vote at 18, you must be 25 to contest most legislative elections.

questions.text: What is an 'Election Manifesto'?
options: A document where a party states its policies and promises
questions.explanation: Parties use manifestos to tell voters what they will do if elected.

questions.text: The 'Election Campaign' in India usually lasts for:
questions.explanation: The official campaign period is between the announcement of the final list of candidates and the date of polling.

questions.text: Which of these is a slogan used in Indian elections?
questions.explanation: These are famous slogans used by different parties to attract voters.

questions.text: According to Election Law, no party or candidate can:
options: Appeal to them in the name of religion
Spend more than the specified limit
questions.explanation: These restrictions are part of the 'Model Code of Conduct' to ensure fair play.

questions.text: Which body conducts elections in India?
questions.explanation: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an independent body that manages the entire election process.

questions.text: Who appoints the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) of India?
questions.explanation: The President appoints the CEC, but once appointed, the CEC is not answerable to the President or government.

questions.text: Elections held for only one constituency to fill a vacancy caused by death or resignation are called:
questions.explanation: By-elections are specific to vacancies between general election cycles.

questions.text: Elections held in all constituencies at the same time, either on the same day or within a few days, are:
questions.explanation: General elections happen every five years for the Lok Sabha and State Assemblies.

questions.text: What is 'Rigging' in an election?
options: Fraud and malpractice to increase votes of a party
questions.explanation: Rigging includes stuffing ballot boxes, intimidation, and impersonation.

questions.text: The EPIC card is the:
questions.explanation: EPIC stands for Election Photo Identity Card.

questions.text: Which of the following is a challenge to free and fair elections in India?
questions.explanation: While India's elections are largely free, these factors still pose significant challenges.

questions.text: What does 'Turnout' mean in an election?
options: The number of candidates who lost
The percentage of eligible voters who actually cast their vote
The time it takes to count votes
questions.explanation: High turnout usually indicates that voters believe their vote matters.

questions.text: In India, who has the power to remove the CEC?
options: Difficult process similar to impeachment of a Judge
questions.explanation: The CEC has a high degree of independence and cannot be easily removed by the executive.

questions.text: A 'Polling Booth' is a place where:
questions.explanation: Polling booths are set up in schools or government buildings on election day.

questions.text: The machine used to record votes is called:
questions.explanation: EVM stands for Electronic Voting Machine.

questions.text: What is 'Booth Capturing'?
options: Taking over a booth by force to cast false votes
questions.explanation: It is an illegal and undemocratic practice.

questions.text: Which state assembly election did Chaudhary Devi Lal lead with the 'Nyaya Yudh' movement?
questions.explanation: Devi Lal led the Lok Dal party to victory in the 1987 Haryana elections.

questions.text: In India, one-third of seats in local government bodies are reserved for:
questions.explanation: This reservation aims to improve female participation in decision making.

questions.text: Elections provide a 'Level Playing Field'. What does this mean?
options: All candidates and parties have an equal opportunity to compete
questions.explanation: The Election Commission ensures that no one has an unfair advantage.

questions.text: In a democracy, the final result of the election is usually accepted as the 'People's ________'.
questions.explanation: Accepting the verdict is a key part of the democratic process.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT an unfair practice in elections?
options: Inclusion of false names in voters' list
Misuse of government facilities by ruling party
questions.explanation: Attending rallies is a normal and legal part of political participation.

questions.text: Voter ID cards are ________.
options: Not yet mandatory, but useful for identification
questions.explanation: Voters can also use other identity proofs like Ration card or Driving license if they don't have an EPIC.

questions.text: A candidate must give a legal declaration of ________ while filing nomination.
questions.explanation: This information allows voters to make an informed choice.

questions.text: The slogan 'Save Democracy' was given by the Janata Party in ________.
questions.explanation: It was used after the Emergency was lifted.

questions.text: The slogan 'Garibi Hatao' was used by which leader?
questions.explanation: Indira Gandhi led the Congress party with this slogan in the 1971 Lok Sabha elections.

questions.text: During elections, government officials on election duty work under the control of:
questions.explanation: This prevents the ruling party from influencing the officers.

questions.text: The number of SC reserved seats in Lok Sabha is ________.
questions.explanation: As per current data, 84 seats are reserved for SC.

questions.text: The number of ST reserved seats in Lok Sabha is ________.
questions.explanation: As per current data, 47 seats are reserved for ST.

questions.text: How much money can a candidate spend for a Lok Sabha election (as per NCERT text limits)?
questions.explanation: There are legal limits on spending; it's 70 lakhs for Lok Sabha and 28 lakhs for Assembly (limits vary by state and time).

questions.text: Why is political competition good?
options: It forces parties to serve the people to stay in power
questions.explanation: Like market competition, it incentivizes politicians to work for the public interest.

questions.text: In India, election results are counted in a ________ manner.
questions.explanation: Counting happens in the presence of agents of all candidates to ensure transparency.
